speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five, March 18, 1997, interview with Esther Gever. So after your parents were-- grandparents were sent to the old age home and you were there with your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father kept working. My mother's health deteriorated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister was a little girl that-- she could still see her mother. But she doesn't remember her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she then? When are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking 1940. 1940, and it-- the same thing happened. My father worked. I took care of the family. The snow in the winter to-- to brave there and get things done wasn't-- it wasn't easy. But it had to be done, and I did it. In 1940, we were told that we are getting out of the labor camp. We were given options where we want to go. There were several locations, which I can't remember. One of them was Tashkent. We did not have the money to go to Tashkent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know whether Tomsk was one of the options that were given free or we only had enough money to go there, but that's all we could do. So we wound up going to Tomsk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came to the station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had no money, no lodgings, no friends. My mother's sick. My sister began to be sick. My father was getting swollen. He was-- from hunger. There's only one thing that was left that could be sold, and I had baby earrings on that were given to me when I was one-year-old. [SIGHS] And my father took the earrings. I willingly gave it to him. And he went to sell it and buy bread. And maybe we could stay overnight somewhere for that money. We waited a whole day. My father isn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the four of us-- just my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which were?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my sister, and I, and my father who went to sell the earrings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Waited the whole day. He isn't there. He's not. I walk in, and I walk out, and we're on the train station in the waiting room. Everyone looks at us. My mother can barely breathe. She was all hunched already. She was so ill. My sister's hungry. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All of a sudden, I see my father coming. Must have been many hours. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I looked at him, and he looked that he would rather die than face us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He sold my earrings. They stole his money-- no earrings, no money, no food. My hungry little sister, my sick mother, [SOBBING] and I'm standing there, and he-- my father's helpless. He got up. I walked out, and I begged. [SOB] I  begged for bread for my family. Yeah, and they stole his papers too. In the Russia without papers, you're a nonentity. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We became homeless people [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] for three months. My mother was deteriorating from-- they wouldn't take her to a hospital without papers. He would go to the police, and they wouldn't talk to him. They thought he is crazy because he started looking like that. He was just-- he was swollen. My sister started getting diarrhea. She was-- we thought she's not going to make it. I was the only one. We slept in hallways. Sometimes in an office, somebody had pity on us. Until one day, a Jewish woman from Leningrad looked at us and started talking to us. And she said, look, I will help you. But you have to denounce your children. In other words, for us to get into a shelter, we're not allowed to know where my parents are. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: The streets, sitting, just pitiful. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she said that if we go to where she's going to send us, the communication between my parents and us, the children, will be through her until my father will be on his feet. And then we can resume family life. We were sent to a orphanage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What the bad thing was about that after we were sent to the orphanage, my sister got separated because of age. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My father-- she-- got-- became employed. And my mother was accepted in the hospital. She arranged everything for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I can't. That orphanage was a-- the children there were runaways, little thieves, a caliber of young people that were kind of dangerous-- hoods, hoodlums. When I came in, I stuck out like a sore thumb. And they started picking on me. The teachers liked me very much. And what they did for me, because they felt that it's not safe for me to be with them, took me into the office to live with them, which was wonderful-- clean and nice. And they cared for me. I was given like four or five cubes of sugar a week. So I would save it and bring it to my mother in the hospital. I was allowed to visit her once a week. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] To go to the bathroom was dangerous because if they caught me in the hallway, they either would kill me-- the boys were ready to rape. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was 11 years old by that time. That was already 1941. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So it was difficult-- very, very difficult. One day, a girl told me that they decided to kill me, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that I'm a privileged character, and they have to kill me. And they would have done it. And I didn't know how I can get out of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I die, who is going to keep the family together? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I decided-- I'm 11 years old. I decided I'm going to tell them where my father is. And therefore they will have to let me out of the orphanage. And it may save my life. And what can happen? And I did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two hours later, a policemen came-- [LAUGHS] [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewee
text: --and brought me to my father. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was-- that was a real [NON-ENGLISH]. It was all men. There must have been probably 100 men. What do you call it, the bunk beds? It's two levels. Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a building?

speaker: interviewee
text: It belonged to the factory [? Svotmetalista, ?] where my father worked. And that was the accommodations given to the workers. So my father slept there. He had his bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the metal factor?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the metal factory. It was-- they were producing ammunition, I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Here I am, a little girl, 100 men. The language, don't even ask. I remember very-- that struck-- something that struck me very funny. There was a man that put something underneath his pillow, and someone stole it from him. And he said a few choice words in Russian. Then he said, damn, it, he said. I had to go miles to steal it. They steal it from underneath my pillow. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's-- that was the Russian lifestyle. One was stealing from the other to survive. And I told my father to go to the office and tell them that your daughter is here. Look, maybe they will give you a better place to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure enough, they interviewed us, and they put us together in a room with a family from Warsaw. There was a woman, a brother, and a husband. So we got the corner with the bed-- better than with 100 men in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got one bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: One bed. The only bad thing about it was my father worked nights a lot and so did her husband. And her brother brought her  in customers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I used to turn to the wall, close my ears, and close my eyes, and just cry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was ashamed to tell my father about this because daughters and fathers don't discuss things like that. And there was no one to talk to. I kept on saying to my father, maybe you can find other work. Maybe-- please look for other work. Why do you have to work in that factory? Look for other work. Maybe you-- you're a skilled man. You're a designer. Maybe-- there have to be some shoe factories somewhere. And he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: He found work in a shoe factory. And that shoe factory gave us accommodations belonging to that outfit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That outfit had a woman shoemaker that was in prison for about 30 years for killing somebody. [LAUGHS] She had a son, and that was her life. She would go kill again for her son. And she was sitting. She was making shoes. And every time she would put the nail in the shoe, she would say a letter-- a four-letter word. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One day I got very sick. My father, again, had to work overtime. And in Russia, you don't work 10 or 12 hours. You work, whatever. It's a-- It was war, and therefore the citizens have to give all they can. And it's never enough. You never got anything for it, but you were there to give.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was alone. My sister's still in the orphanage. I walk-- winter. It was across a river. If I ever remember that name-- I could kick myself for not remembering. But the river was frozen in the winter. Therefore I had access, and I would go there and stand.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't allowed to see her or even acknowledge my presence. But I knew what time they would play in the yard, and I would go there and stand and look and see her. She looked so lonely. [SOB] One day-- during the summer, I couldn't do it because I couldn't get there. I-- well, I got sick one day. And I was so sick. I was just-- I felt I'm dying. There was no one to give me even a cup of tea. I was just praying to God [SOB] that He should let me live so that I can get my sister out-- [SOB] --from the orphanage. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I recovered somehow. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And again, the same-- I was chopping wood, sawing wood. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And life was very difficult. It was cold. It was hunger. We used to slice up radishes. And they had in Siberia very big radishes. And if-- whatever you could-- potato peels, grass, anything. But those radishes, you would slice them. And then we put them on the stove on the iron to bake them-- anything. Hunger was a constant companion. That was constant. Lice, hunger was always present. Finally, we were lucky enough to get an apartment. And I had one room all to ourselves. We got that apartment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said to my father, it's time to get my sister back. He said, yes, we should. Meanwhile, my mother's health deteriorated so. And daily, I was visited. And she didn't fight it anymore. She didn't care. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: One day she said she would like to eat something. And I said, what would you like to eat? And she said, you know, I would love to have some hard-boiled eggs-- luxury. I said, you know what, Mom? I'm going to get it for you. And I called my father. I call-- I went to see him, and I said, you better go to see Mother. I don't like the way she looks. I went to look for eggs. And sure enough, I found eggs. And I came to the hospital. It was too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] She's-- she died April 26, 1943-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] [BACKGROUND NOISE] Suffered unbelievably, what she went through. How lonely and desperate she must have felt at times. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I said, now it's time to get my sister. Whatever's left of us, let's salvage it. We got-- and I had to do it all. A little bit difficulty, a little paperwork, and red tape-- things in Russia and so forth. What I found out was that during the last summer month my sister was adopted. We didn't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was adopted by a colonel. And he got killed at the front. When he got killed, his wife decided to give her back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was how she came back to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your mother buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomsk-- no plot, piece of wood, three people walking [SOBBING] behind the casket. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And-- [SIGHS] --life ended. [BACKGROUND NOISE] A few months later, they decided to bring us to [? Kavkaz, ?] [INAUDIBLE] [? Kavkaz-- ?] northern [? Kavkaz ?]-- [? Krasnodarski ?] [? Krai. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. It was a farm town. They were all farmers-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] cooperative farmers-- [RUSSIAN] people. They-- some of them had little private houses. And I wound up-- I was with a Russian woman that we rented a little room from. And she was very nice to me. [BACKGROUND NOISE] She taught me how to knit socks. And I was sitting every night till 2 o'clock in the morning knitting socks for people and getting paid with milk. During the day-- during the day, I used-- I took care of whatever. I used to go and gather, which was called [? burion. ?] [? Burion ?] was the-- is something that you used for wood in the stove because it was flat land and there were very few trees. There were bushes and-- and-- and what do you call them, the weeds that dried up. And the weeds were the [? burion. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I learned to take a rope and make a big, big bundle, as much as I could tie it up so that I could cook my-- our dinner. What about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't go to school. My sister was there. I had to take care of her. I had to take care of my father. I had tutoring because the teachers liked me. And whenever I could, I would attend, but it wasn't enough. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I plowed. They gave us earth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't remember how much it was-- [RUSSIAN] or something like that. I don't remember. It was like my apartment probably, the size of my apartment. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I sowed corn. I watered whatever I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I saw what other people did, and I did it, too. And then I used to take the corn, dry it in the sun, then with a stick, take off the kernels and blow it in the wind, out-- clean it out as much as possible. And then I went to a mill to grind it for flour. And the rafa was the kasha that we cooked. And we weren't that hungry anymore. And the mill was two stones. You know, you can see this only in pre-historic movies. It took extraordinary strength to go in a circle. You would push it. You could walk literally in a circle and push those stones. You poured the corn in. And on the bottom you had that to hamper, and it would come down and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You did it yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did it myself-- well, who is going to do it? It also to be noted that the floors were from lime. And we needed cow manure [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to make it look pretty and nice for the weekend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us how that was done?

speaker: interviewee
text: You  made-- it was quite artistic because you took lime, and you made the beautiful border. And then the cow manure was diluted with water and with a rag. And you made circles. It was really quite intricate work. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did that. What  did that do for the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: It protected-- it didn't-- it protected it. It finished it. It was like a finish. That it shouldn't get scarred or the earth-- it shouldn't-- it kept it intact. I don't remember exactly. But it-- it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it still dusty after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, when it dried out it was dusty. But what's a little dust in the-- under those circumstances? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I made friends with the Russian girl that I loved. And she was my only other person that I could talk to. There was no one. There wasn't another Jewish family, not another-- not another-- no one I could talk to. She was Russian. She was different. She was very delicate and very sentimental. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marusia. Marusia Butsaieva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about Jewish holidays? Did you know when was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing. Nothing-- absolutely no contact whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you and Marusia have any [INAUDIBLE] things you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: We read books. I was writing poetry. I did not have paper. I mean, I wrote over old newspapers or birch bark.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russian. I was very fluent. I was very, very fluent and very good at it. No one could recognize that I wasn't born in Russia. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now.
